title: Safe Intravenous PHENYTOIN Loading and NARROW-THERAPEUTIC-INDEX Monitoring in a 27-Year-Old Woman with Epilepsy Admitted After a Breakthrough Generalized Tonic-Clonic Seizure from Medication Nonadherence: NEVER Mix in Dextrose (Saline + Inline Filter Only), Slow Rate with Continuous Cardiac/BP Monitoring, Purple-Glove and Extravasation Vigilance, Free-Phenytoin Interpretation in Hypoalbuminemia, and Adverse-Effect + Enzyme-Inducer Drug-Interaction Teaching
subtitle: PHENYTOIN is a first-line antiseizure (anticonvulsant) medication with a NARROW THERAPEUTIC INDEX (NTI) — the gap between an effective and a toxic dose is small, so it requires careful DOSING and serum-LEVEL MONITORING. The therapeutic TOTAL serum level is about 10-20 mcg/mL (free/unbound ~1-2 mcg/mL). TOXICITY is dose-related and progresses predictably with rising levels: NYSTAGMUS appears first (often >20), then ATAXIA, slurred speech, and DIPLOPIA (~>30), then confusion, lethargy, and ultimately coma/seizures at very high levels (~>40). A crucial interpretation point: phenytoin is HIGHLY PROTEIN-BOUND (to albumin), and only the FREE (unbound) fraction is active. In HYPOALBUMINEMIA (or renal failure/uremia, or with displacing drugs), the TOTAL level UNDER-ESTIMATES the active free drug — a patient can be clinically TOXIC despite a 'normal' total level — so obtain a FREE phenytoin level or use a correction (e.g., Sheiner-Tozer) when albumin is low. INTRAVENOUS phenytoin is HIGH-RISK to administer: it is INCOMPATIBLE WITH DEXTROSE (it PRECIPITATES in dextrose-containing fluids and forms crystals/microemboli), so it is diluted/flushed with NORMAL SALINE ONLY and given through an INLINE FILTER. It must be infused SLOWLY — NOT faster than 50 mg/min in adults (and ≤25 mg/min in older adults or those with cardiac disease) — because rapid infusion causes HYPOTENSION, BRADYCARDIA, heart block, and asystole (largely from the propylene-glycol diluent). Continuous CARDIAC and BLOOD-PRESSURE MONITORING is required during loading; the rate is slowed or stopped for hypotension/bradycardia. Phenytoin is a vesicant: extravasation/infiltration can cause 'PURPLE GLOVE SYNDROME' (limb edema, discoloration, and ischemia), so use a PATENT, large-bore IV in a LARGE vein (avoid small hand/wrist veins), verify blood return, and never IV-push undiluted phenytoin into a small line. FOSPHENYTOIN (a water-soluble prodrug, dosed in 'phenytoin-equivalents'/PE) is often preferred because it is better tolerated, can be given faster and IM, is compatible with saline AND dextrose, and causes less site irritation. CHRONIC/oral adverse effects to teach: GINGIVAL HYPERPLASIA (overgrowth of the gums — meticulous oral hygiene, soft toothbrush, regular dental care), HIRSUTISM and coarsening of facial features, RASH that may herald STEVENS-JOHNSON SYNDROME / DRESS (a serious rash → STOP the drug and notify the provider immediately), folate/vitamin-D/calcium effects (megaloblastic anemia; osteomalacia/osteoporosis with long-term use), and gait/balance changes if levels rise. DRUG INTERACTIONS: phenytoin is a potent hepatic ENZYME INDUCER (CYP450) that REDUCES the effectiveness of many drugs — notably HORMONAL CONTRACEPTIVES (use an ADDITIONAL/backup nonhormonal method), warfarin, and others — and its own level is changed by many drugs (e.g., some antibiotics and antifungals raise it). It is a known TERATOGEN (fetal hydantoin syndrome), so women of childbearing potential need contraception counseling and preconception planning with FOLIC ACID. ADHERENCE is critical: abrupt discontinuation can precipitate status epilepticus, so the drug is NEVER stopped suddenly; missed doses and drug interactions (here, a recent interacting course plus running out of medication) are common precipitants of breakthrough seizures. NURSING ROLE: ensure post-ictal airway/safety and seizure precautions; recognize the breakthrough seizure and the subtherapeutic level; administer IV phenytoin SAFELY (saline only — NEVER dextrose; inline filter; slow rate ≤50 mg/min with continuous cardiac/BP monitoring; large patent vein with extravasation/purple-glove vigilance; consider fosphenytoin); MONITOR serum levels and interpret them in light of ALBUMIN (free level when low); watch for dose-related toxicity (nystagmus → ataxia → confusion) and for serious rash; and TEACH adverse effects (gingival hyperplasia/oral care, report rash), drug interactions (enzyme inducer — additional contraception, warfarin), teratogenicity/folic acid, and strict ADHERENCE (never stop abruptly; consistent levels; follow-up labs).

patient.name: J. Alvarez
patient.code: Full code; goal = ensure post-ictal safety, safely administer the IV phenytoin (fosphenytoin) load, monitor the narrow-therapeutic-index level (interpreted with albumin), prevent infusion/extravasation injury and toxicity, and teach adverse effects, interactions, and adherence
patient.admitDate: A 27-year-old woman with a known seizure disorder (generalized epilepsy) maintained on oral PHENYTOIN is brought in by EMS after a witnessed GENERALIZED TONIC-CLONIC SEIZURE at work. She reports she RAN OUT of her phenytoin a week ago and recently finished an antibiotic course; she is now postictal — drowsy and confused but protecting her airway. Her serum phenytoin level is SUBTHERAPEUTIC at 6.2 mcg/mL (therapeutic ~10-20) and her serum albumin is LOW at 2.8 g/dL (which means the total level under-represents the active free drug). The provider orders an IV phenytoin/fosphenytoin LOADING dose and resumption of oral maintenance. The nurse must give this NTI medication SAFELY — saline only (NEVER dextrose), inline filter, slow rate (≤50 mg/min) with continuous cardiac and blood-pressure monitoring, a large patent vein with purple-glove/extravasation vigilance — then monitor levels (free level given low albumin), watch for dose-related toxicity, and provide thorough teaching.
patient.room: Telemetry/ED-to-med-surg with continuous cardiac and blood-pressure monitoring; IV fosphenytoin/phenytoin load in NORMAL SALINE via infusion pump with inline filter through a large patent vein; seizure precautions; serial phenytoin and free-phenytoin/albumin levels; interdisciplinary team (provider, bedside RN, pharmacy, neurology); monitoring for hypotension/bradycardia, extravasation/purple glove, dose-related toxicity, and serious rash

vitals.temp: 37.1°C; postictal after a witnessed GENERALIZED TONIC-CLONIC seizure — drowsy/confused but maintaining her airway; tongue-bite noted; ran OUT of phenytoin ~1 week ago + recent interacting antibiotic course; serum phenytoin SUBTHERAPEUTIC at 6.2 mcg/mL (therapeutic 10-20); albumin LOW 2.8 g/dL — seizure precautions in place

37.0°C; before IV phenytoin: verified the order/dose; confirmed a PATENT, large-bore IV in a LARGE vein with blood return (avoid small hand veins); confirmed NORMAL SALINE only (NEVER dextrose — phenytoin precipitates) with an INLINE FILTER; placed on continuous CARDIAC + BLOOD-PRESSURE monitoring; baseline level + albumin/free-level obtained

37.0°C; fosphenytoin/phenytoin LOAD infused in NORMAL SALINE via PUMP through an inline filter at a SLOW rate (NOT >50 mg/min adult; ≤25 mg/min if older/cardiac) — never IV-pushed undiluted; continuous ECG + frequent BP; IV site watched for pain/discoloration (purple-glove risk)

37.0°C; WARNING pattern if phenytoin is infused TOO FAST — HYPOTENSION, BRADYCARDIA, heart block, and even asystole (largely from the propylene-glycol diluent) — and/or arm pain/discoloration from extravasation (PURPLE GLOVE); response = SLOW or STOP the infusion, support BP, and protect the limb — underscoring the rate limit and monitoring

37.0°C; if the level climbs too high, DOSE-RELATED TOXICITY appears in order — NYSTAGMUS first, then ATAXIA / slurred speech / DIPLOPIA, then confusion/lethargy; with albumin LOW, a 'normal' TOTAL level can still be toxic — so a FREE phenytoin level guides interpretation; watch for serious RASH (SJS/DRESS → stop drug)

37.0°C; level corrected to therapeutic (~15 mcg/mL, free level appropriate), no further seizures, no hypotension/arrhythmia, IV site intact; taught gingival hygiene + report rash, enzyme-inducer interactions (ADDITIONAL contraception, warfarin), folic acid/teratogenicity, and NEVER stopping the drug abruptly

labs.n: PRESENTATION + recognizing the breakthrough seizure and SUBTHERAPEUTIC phenytoin
labs.v: J. Alvarez, 27, with known generalized epilepsy on oral PHENYTOIN, presents POSTICTAL after a witnessed GENERALIZED TONIC-CLONIC seizure. She RAN OUT of her phenytoin ~1 week ago and recently finished an antibiotic course (common precipitants — nonadherence and drug interactions). She is drowsy/confused but PROTECTING HER AIRWAY, with a tongue-bite. Her serum phenytoin is SUBTHERAPEUTIC at 6.2 mcg/mL (therapeutic ~10-20) and her serum ALBUMIN is LOW at 2.8 g/dL. The immediate priorities are POSTICTAL SAFETY (airway, side-lying, seizure precautions, injury survey) and recognizing that a subtherapeutic level from missed doses explains the breakthrough seizure, requiring a phenytoin LOAD and resumption of maintenance. Because phenytoin is a NARROW-THERAPEUTIC-INDEX drug given IV, the nurse's central task is to administer it SAFELY and to interpret/monitor the level correctly.
labs.r: Breakthrough GENERALIZED TONIC-CLONIC seizure in known epilepsy; precipitants = nonadherence (ran out) + interacting antibiotic. SUBTHERAPEUTIC phenytoin 6.2 (goal 10-20). Albumin LOW 2.8. PRIORITIES: postictal airway/safety + seizure precautions; recognize subtherapeutic level → load + resume maintenance. RN role: ensure safety + recognize the cause.

labs.n: Phenytoin is NARROW-THERAPEUTIC-INDEX — level 10-20; toxicity progression; FREE level with low albumin
labs.v: Phenytoin has a NARROW THERAPEUTIC INDEX, so serum LEVELS are monitored: therapeutic TOTAL ~10-20 mcg/mL (free/unbound ~1-2). TOXICITY is dose-related and progresses PREDICTABLY: NYSTAGMUS first (often >20), then ATAXIA, slurred speech, and DIPLOPIA (~>30), then confusion, lethargy, and ultimately coma/paradoxical seizures (~>40). Critically, phenytoin is HIGHLY PROTEIN-BOUND (albumin) and only the FREE fraction is active; in HYPOALBUMINEMIA (her albumin is 2.8), uremia, or with displacing drugs, the TOTAL level UNDER-ESTIMATES the active drug — a patient can be clinically TOXIC at a 'normal' total level. Therefore obtain a FREE phenytoin level (or apply a correction such as Sheiner-Tozer) when albumin is low, and always correlate the level with the CLINICAL exam (look for nystagmus/ataxia) rather than trusting a number alone.
labs.r: NTI drug → monitor LEVELS. Therapeutic total 10-20 (free 1-2). Toxicity order: NYSTAGMUS → ATAXIA/diplopia/slurred speech → confusion/coma. Highly PROTEIN-BOUND → in LOW ALBUMIN (or uremia) total UNDER-estimates active free drug; can be toxic at 'normal' total → get FREE level/correction + correlate clinically. RN role: monitor + correctly interpret the level.

labs.n: IV phenytoin administration safety — SALINE only (NEVER dextrose), inline filter, slow rate, cardiac/BP monitoring, purple glove; fosphenytoin
labs.v: IV phenytoin is HIGH-RISK. It is INCOMPATIBLE WITH DEXTROSE — it PRECIPITATES/crystallizes in dextrose-containing fluids (microemboli risk) — so it is diluted/flushed with NORMAL SALINE ONLY and infused through an INLINE FILTER. It must be given SLOWLY: NOT faster than 50 mg/min in adults (≤25 mg/min in older adults or cardiac disease), because rapid infusion causes HYPOTENSION, BRADYCARDIA, heart block, and asystole (largely from the propylene-glycol diluent). Continuous CARDIAC and BLOOD-PRESSURE monitoring is required during the load; SLOW or STOP the infusion for hypotension/bradycardia. Phenytoin is a VESICANT — extravasation can cause 'PURPLE GLOVE SYNDROME' (limb edema, purple discoloration, ischemia) — so use a PATENT, LARGE-bore vein (avoid small hand/wrist veins), confirm blood return, and never push undiluted drug into a small line. FOSPHENYTOIN (water-soluble prodrug, dosed in PE) is often preferred: it tolerates faster IV and IM administration, is compatible with saline AND dextrose, and causes less venous irritation.
labs.r: IV phenytoin: NORMAL SALINE ONLY — NEVER dextrose (precipitates) + INLINE FILTER. Rate ≤50 mg/min adult (≤25 if older/cardiac) — too fast → HYPOTENSION/BRADYCARDIA/asystole. CONTINUOUS cardiac + BP monitoring; slow/stop for instability. VESICANT → PURPLE GLOVE → large patent vein, blood return, no small-vein push. FOSPHENYTOIN (PE) safer/faster, saline+dextrose compatible, IM-capable. RN role: administer IV phenytoin safely.

labs.n: Adverse effects + DRUG INTERACTIONS (enzyme inducer) + teratogenicity
labs.v: CHRONIC/oral adverse effects to teach: GINGIVAL HYPERPLASIA (gum overgrowth — meticulous oral hygiene, soft toothbrush, flossing, regular DENTAL care); HIRSUTISM and coarsening of facial features; a RASH that may herald STEVENS-JOHNSON SYNDROME / DRESS (a serious, potentially fatal reaction → STOP the drug and notify the provider immediately); folate/vitamin-D/calcium effects (megaloblastic ANEMIA; long-term OSTEOMALACIA/osteoporosis); and gait/balance changes if levels rise. DRUG INTERACTIONS: phenytoin is a potent hepatic ENZYME INDUCER (CYP450) that REDUCES the effectiveness of many drugs — notably HORMONAL CONTRACEPTIVES (she must use an ADDITIONAL/backup nonhormonal method to avoid unintended pregnancy), WARFARIN, and others — while its own level can be RAISED by certain antibiotics/antifungals/isoniazid or lowered by others. It is a known TERATOGEN (fetal hydantoin syndrome): women of childbearing potential need contraception counseling and preconception planning with FOLIC ACID. ADHERENCE is essential — abrupt discontinuation can trigger STATUS EPILEPTICUS — so the drug is NEVER stopped suddenly, doses are taken consistently, and levels/labs are followed.
labs.r: TEACH adverse effects: GINGIVAL HYPERPLASIA (oral/dental hygiene), hirsutism, RASH → SJS/DRESS (STOP + notify), megaloblastic anemia (folate), osteomalacia. INTERACTIONS: ENZYME INDUCER → ↓ hormonal contraceptives (use ADDITIONAL method) + ↓ warfarin; own level raised/lowered by other drugs. TERATOGEN (fetal hydantoin) → contraception + folic acid + preconception planning. NEVER stop abruptly (status epilepticus). RN role: teach effects/interactions/adherence.

labs.n: Nursing role + teaching; Pertinent NEGATIVES + protective factors
labs.v: PATIENT TEACHING: take phenytoin CONSISTENTLY and NEVER stop abruptly (refill ahead; abrupt stop → status epilepticus); maintain meticulous ORAL HYGIENE and see the dentist for gingival hyperplasia; REPORT a rash, fever, mouth sores, or blistering immediately (SJS/DRESS); use an ADDITIONAL nonhormonal contraceptive method (enzyme inducer reduces birth-control efficacy) and discuss preconception planning + FOLIC ACID given teratogenicity; report excessive sleepiness, unsteady gait, double vision, or involuntary eye movements (toxicity); avoid alcohol; keep follow-up LEVELS/labs; and tell every provider you take phenytoin (many interactions, e.g., warfarin). PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice): the nurse did NOT mix phenytoin in dextrose or omit the inline filter; did NOT IV-push it rapidly or exceed 50 mg/min; did NOT use a tiny hand vein or ignore site pain/discoloration; did NOT trust a 'normal' total level despite low albumin; and did NOT advise stopping the drug once seizure-free. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: postictal airway/safety and seizure precautions; saline-only dilution with inline filter; slow, pump-controlled infusion with continuous cardiac/BP monitoring; large patent vein with extravasation vigilance (or fosphenytoin); level monitoring interpreted with albumin/free level; toxicity and rash surveillance; and thorough teaching that secures ADHERENCE. EXPECTED outcome: the level is corrected to therapeutic, seizures stop, no hypotension/arrhythmia or extravasation injury occurs, toxicity and serious rash are avoided, and the patient understands safe self-management. NURSING ROLE: ensure safety; recognize the subtherapeutic level; administer IV phenytoin safely; monitor/interpret levels; watch for toxicity and rash; and educate.
labs.r: TEACH: consistent dosing/NEVER stop abruptly (status epilepticus), oral/dental hygiene (gingival hyperplasia), REPORT rash/fever/blisters (SJS/DRESS), ADDITIONAL contraception + folic acid (enzyme inducer + teratogen), report sleepiness/ataxia/diplopia/nystagmus, avoid alcohol, follow-up levels, disclose drug to all providers. NEGATIVES (correct practice): no dextrose/no missing filter, no rapid push/>50 mg/min, no tiny-vein/ignoring site, didn't trust total level w/ low albumin, didn't stop when seizure-free. PROTECTIVE: safety/precautions, saline+filter, slow pump + cardiac/BP monitoring, large vein/fosphenytoin, level+albumin interpretation, toxicity/rash watch, teaching. RN role: safe care + monitoring + education.

labs.v: Total phenytoin 6.2 mcg/mL — SUBTHERAPEUTIC (therapeutic ~10-20 mcg/mL). Consistent with missed doses (ran out ~1 week ago) and explains the breakthrough seizure. Supports a LOADING dose then resumption of maintenance, with a follow-up level to confirm therapeutic range.
labs.r: Phenytoin TOTAL 6.2 = SUBTHERAPEUTIC (goal 10-20) → breakthrough seizure from nonadherence → load + maintenance + recheck level.

labs.v: Albumin 2.8 g/dL — LOW (normal ~3.5-5.0). Because phenytoin is highly protein-bound, low albumin means the TOTAL level under-represents the ACTIVE free drug; obtain a FREE phenytoin level or apply a correction, and correlate with the clinical exam — the patient could be relatively higher in free drug than the total suggests.
labs.r: Albumin LOW 2.8 → total phenytoin UNDER-estimates active free fraction → get FREE level/correction + correlate clinically.

labs.v: Post-load phenytoin ~15 mcg/mL with an appropriate FREE level — now THERAPEUTIC (10-20). No further seizure activity. Confirms effective, safe correction and guides ongoing maintenance dosing.
labs.r: Post-load level ~15 = THERAPEUTIC; free level appropriate; no further seizures → effective correction; continue maintenance + monitor.

labs.v: Baseline CBC and liver enzymes within normal limits (no megaloblastic anemia, no leukopenia, AST/ALT normal). Establishes a reference before/at start of therapy given phenytoin's hematologic (megaloblastic anemia/folate) and hepatic effects, and supports ongoing surveillance during chronic therapy.
labs.r: Baseline CBC + LFTs NORMAL → reference for phenytoin's hematologic (megaloblastic anemia) + hepatic effects; monitor over time.

labs.n: Cardiac monitor + blood pressure during infusion
labs.v: On telemetry during the controlled-rate fosphenytoin/phenytoin load: sinus rhythm without bradycardia or heart block, blood pressure stable (no hypotension), and the IV site without pain, swelling, or discoloration. Confirms the rate limit, saline-only/filter technique, large-vein access, and continuous monitoring are protecting the patient.
labs.r: Telemetry during load: stable rhythm + BP, IV site intact → rate limit + saline/filter + large vein + monitoring working. Watch for bradycardia/hypotension/extravasation if any change.

nursesNote: ED→telemetry — J. Alvarez, 27, known generalized EPILEPSY on oral PHENYTOIN, brought in POSTICTAL after a witnessed GENERALIZED TONIC-CLONIC seizure. RAN OUT of phenytoin ~1 week ago + recent interacting antibiotic course (nonadherence + interaction = classic precipitants). Drowsy/confused but PROTECTING AIRWAY; tongue-bite; seizure precautions/side-lying. Phenytoin SUBTHERAPEUTIC 6.2 (goal 10-20); ALBUMIN LOW 2.8 → total UNDER-estimates active FREE drug (get free level/correction; correlate clinically). Plan: IV fosphenytoin/phenytoin LOAD + resume maintenance. PHENYTOIN = NARROW-THERAPEUTIC-INDEX → monitor LEVELS; toxicity progression NYSTAGMUS → ATAXIA/diplopia/slurred speech → confusion/coma. IV ADMIN SAFETY: NORMAL SALINE ONLY — NEVER dextrose (precipitates) + INLINE FILTER; rate ≤50 mg/min adult (≤25 older/cardiac) — too fast → HYPOTENSION/BRADYCARDIA/heart block/asystole (propylene-glycol diluent); CONTINUOUS cardiac + BP monitoring (slow/stop for instability); VESICANT → PURPLE GLOVE syndrome → large patent vein, blood return, no small-vein push; FOSPHENYTOIN (PE) safer/faster, saline+dextrose compatible, IM-capable. ADVERSE EFFECTS: GINGIVAL HYPERPLASIA (oral/dental hygiene), hirsutism, RASH → SJS/DRESS (STOP + notify), megaloblastic anemia (folate), osteomalacia. INTERACTIONS: ENZYME INDUCER → ↓ hormonal contraceptives (use ADDITIONAL method) + ↓ warfarin; level raised by some antibiotics/antifungals/INH. TERATOGEN (fetal hydantoin) → contraception + folic acid + preconception planning. ADHERENCE: NEVER stop abruptly (status epilepticus); refill ahead; follow-up levels; disclose phenytoin to all providers; avoid alcohol; report sleepiness/ataxia/diplopia/nystagmus + rash/fever/blisters. NEGATIVES (correct practice): no dextrose/no missing filter, no rapid push/>50 mg/min, no tiny-vein/ignoring site discoloration, didn't trust total level w/ low albumin, didn't advise stopping when seizure-free. Outcome: post-load level ~15 (therapeutic, free level appropriate), no further seizures, no hypotension/arrhythmia/extravasation, site intact, patient teaches-back self-management.
providerOrders: ADMIT: telemetry with continuous cardiac and blood-pressure monitoring; postictal after breakthrough generalized tonic-clonic seizure; seizure precautions
DIAGNOSIS: subtherapeutic phenytoin (6.2) from nonadherence/interaction — load and resume maintenance; protect airway/safety

LABS/DIAGNOSTICS: total phenytoin now and after loading; FREE phenytoin level and serum albumin (low albumin → interpret free level); CBC, comprehensive metabolic panel/hepatic panel; pregnancy test; consider neurology consult
Dilute/flush with NORMAL SALINE ONLY — do NOT use dextrose-containing fluids (phenytoin precipitates); use an INLINE FILTER
Infuse via pump SLOWLY — do NOT exceed 50 mg/min in adults (≤25 mg/min if older adult or cardiac disease); never IV-push undiluted phenytoin
Administer through a PATENT, LARGE-bore vein with blood return (avoid small hand/wrist veins); monitor the site for pain/swelling/discoloration (purple-glove/extravasation)
Fosphenytoin (dosed in phenytoin-equivalents/PE) preferred when available — better tolerated, saline/dextrose compatible, faster/IM-capable
Continuous cardiac monitoring and frequent blood pressure during the load; SLOW or STOP for hypotension, bradycardia, or heart block

Watch for dose-related toxicity (nystagmus → ataxia/diplopia/slurred speech → confusion) and interpret levels with albumin/free level; monitor for serious rash (SJS/DRESS) and stop the drug if it occurs
RESUME oral maintenance phenytoin; do NOT discontinue abruptly

EDUCATION: adherence (never stop abruptly), oral/dental hygiene for gingival hyperplasia, report rash/fever/blistering immediately, ADDITIONAL nonhormonal contraception + folic acid (enzyme inducer + teratogen), report sleepiness/unsteadiness/double vision, avoid alcohol, disclose phenytoin to all providers, and keep follow-up levels

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters for this patient on IV phenytoin?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings/considerations that are clinically significant.

opts.text: Postictal after a generalized tonic-clonic seizure but protecting her airway — maintain seizure precautions, side-lying positioning, and an injury survey
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — postictal safety/airway and seizure precautions come first. RN role: ensure safety.

opts.text: Phenytoin level 6.2 mcg/mL is subtherapeutic, and she ran out of her medication ~1 week ago plus finished an interacting antibiotic — explaining the breakthrough seizure
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — a subtherapeutic level from nonadherence/interaction is the cause and the reason for loading. RN role: recognize the cause.

opts.text: IV phenytoin must be given in NORMAL SALINE only (never dextrose), through an inline filter, slowly (≤50 mg/min) with continuous cardiac and BP monitoring
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the core IV administration-safety rules. RN role: administer safely.

opts.text: Albumin is low (2.8 g/dL), so the total phenytoin level under-represents the active free drug — obtain a free level and correlate with the exam
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — hypoalbuminemia changes how the NTI level is interpreted. RN role: interpret the level correctly.

opts.text: Phenytoin is a vesicant — use a large patent vein and watch the IV site for pain/discoloration (purple-glove/extravasation)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — extravasation can cause purple glove syndrome/limb injury. RN role: protect the IV site.

opts.text: Plan teaching on adverse effects (gingival hyperplasia, report rash), enzyme-inducer interactions (additional contraception, warfarin), folic acid, and never stopping abruptly
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — teaching secures adherence and prevents harm. RN role: educate.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA — documented before medication administration
opts.rat: Allergy documentation is routine before medications. Not the central cue.

opts.text: Because she keeps seizing, give a rapid IV phenytoin push to stop the seizure as fast as possible
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG and dangerous — rapid IV phenytoin causes hypotension, bradycardia, and asystole; it must be infused slowly (≤50 mg/min), never pushed undiluted.

opts.text: Mix the phenytoin in a dextrose (D5W) solution to dilute it for infusion
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — phenytoin PRECIPITATES in dextrose; it is diluted/flushed with NORMAL SALINE only and given through an inline filter.

opts.text: Phenytoin has a narrow therapeutic index (therapeutic ~10-20 mcg/mL), so serum levels are monitored to keep it effective without toxicity
opts.rat: CORRECT — NTI drug requiring level monitoring. Foundation for safe dosing.

opts.text: Dose-related toxicity progresses predictably — nystagmus first, then ataxia/diplopia/slurred speech, then confusion and lethargy
opts.rat: CORRECT — the toxicity progression. Foundation for recognizing rising levels.

opts.text: Because phenytoin is highly protein-bound, low albumin makes the total level under-estimate the active free drug — a patient can be toxic at a 'normal' total level
opts.rat: CORRECT — the free-level/albumin interpretation point. Foundation for correct monitoring.

opts.text: IV phenytoin is incompatible with dextrose (precipitates), is given in saline through an inline filter, slowly (≤50 mg/min), with continuous cardiac/BP monitoring
opts.rat: CORRECT — the cardinal IV administration rules. Foundation for preventing fatal infusion reactions.

opts.text: Phenytoin is a potent enzyme inducer that reduces the effectiveness of hormonal contraceptives and warfarin, and it is a teratogen requiring folic acid/contraception counseling
opts.rat: CORRECT — key drug interactions and teratogenicity. Foundation for teaching.

opts.text: A new rash with fever or mucosal involvement may signal Stevens-Johnson syndrome/DRESS and requires stopping the drug and notifying the provider
opts.rat: CORRECT — serious cutaneous reaction. Foundation for safety surveillance.

opts.text: Phenytoin can be safely mixed in dextrose solutions and given by rapid IV push when a seizure is ongoing
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — it precipitates in dextrose and rapid push causes hypotension/bradycardia/asystole; saline only, slow infusion (≤50 mg/min).

opts.text: A 'normal' total phenytoin level always rules out toxicity, even when the albumin is low
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — with low albumin the free (active) level can be high despite a normal total; obtain a free level and correlate clinically.

opts.text: Ensure postictal airway/safety, seizure precautions, and continuous cardiac/BP monitoring
opts.rat: #1 — Airway/safety and monitoring protect the patient before and during drug administration. RN role: ensure safety.

opts.text: Complete pre-administration safety checks — verify the order/dose, confirm a large patent vein, saline-only with inline filter, and baseline level/albumin
opts.rat: #2 — These safeguards must precede the infusion to prevent precipitation, extravasation, and misinterpretation. RN role: pre-administration checks.

opts.text: Administer the phenytoin/fosphenytoin load SAFELY — saline, inline filter, slow rate (≤50 mg/min), never pushed, with monitoring
opts.rat: #3 — Safe administration of the high-risk NTI drug prevents fatal infusion reactions. RN role: safe administration.

opts.text: Monitor for infusion reactions (hypotension/bradycardia), extravasation/purple glove, and dose-related toxicity, and recheck the level (with free level)
opts.rat: #4 — Ongoing monitoring catches cardiovascular reactions, site injury, and toxicity. RN role: monitor + recheck.

opts.text: Educate on adherence, adverse effects, drug interactions, contraception/folic acid, and reporting rash
opts.rat: #5 — Education sustains safe therapy and prevents recurrence. RN role: patient teaching.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for safe phenytoin therapy.

opts.text: Dilute/flush the phenytoin with normal saline and give it through an inline filter via an infusion pump
opts.rat: Saline-only with a filter prevents precipitation/microemboli. RN role: correct diluent + filter.

opts.text: Limit the infusion rate to ≤50 mg/min (≤25 mg/min if older/cardiac) with continuous cardiac and blood-pressure monitoring
opts.rat: Rate limits + monitoring prevent hypotension, bradycardia, and asystole. RN role: respect the rate limit.

opts.text: Use a large, patent vein with blood return and watch the site for pain/swelling/discoloration; consider fosphenytoin
opts.rat: Protects against extravasation/purple glove; fosphenytoin is better tolerated. RN role: protect the IV site.

opts.text: Obtain a free phenytoin level (or correction) given the low albumin and correlate the level with the clinical exam
opts.rat: Low albumin makes the total level misleading; the free level guides care. RN role: interpret the level correctly.

opts.text: Teach meticulous oral hygiene/dental care for gingival hyperplasia and to report any rash, fever, or mouth blistering immediately
opts.rat: Addresses gingival hyperplasia and the serious SJS/DRESS risk. RN role: teach adverse-effect monitoring.

opts.text: Teach the use of an additional nonhormonal contraceptive method and folic acid because phenytoin reduces birth-control efficacy and is teratogenic
opts.rat: Enzyme induction lowers contraceptive efficacy; teratogenicity warrants folic acid/planning. RN role: interaction + teratogen teaching.

opts.text: Mix the phenytoin in D5W to dilute it before infusing
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — phenytoin precipitates in dextrose; use normal saline only with an inline filter.

opts.text: Give a rapid IV push of undiluted phenytoin to abort the seizure quickly
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — rapid administration causes hypotension, bradycardia, and asystole; infuse slowly (≤50 mg/min), never push undiluted.

opts.text: Run the infusion through a small hand vein and ignore mild site discoloration to avoid restarting the IV
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — phenytoin is a vesicant; a small vein and ignored discoloration risk purple glove syndrome/limb ischemia.

opts.text: Reassure the patient that a normal total phenytoin level rules out toxicity despite her albumin of 2.8
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — with low albumin the active free level can be high at a normal total; obtain a free level and correlate clinically.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Ensure postictal airway/safety and seizure precautions, and place the patient on continuous cardiac and BP monitoring
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Safety and monitoring frame the entire administration. RN role: ensure safety + monitor.

opts.text: Complete pre-administration checks — verify the order/dose, establish a large patent vein, confirm saline-only with an inline filter, and obtain baseline level/albumin
opts.rat: #2 — Second. These safeguards must precede the infusion. RN role: pre-administration checks.

opts.text: Administer the phenytoin/fosphenytoin load in saline by pump at ≤50 mg/min through the filter — never pushed — with cardiac/BP monitoring
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Safe, slow, monitored administration prevents fatal reactions. RN role: safe administration.

opts.text: Monitor for hypotension/bradycardia and extravasation during the load, watch for dose-related toxicity, and recheck the level (free level) afterward
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Ongoing monitoring detects cardiovascular reactions, site injury, and toxicity. RN role: monitor + recheck.

opts.text: Teach adherence, adverse effects, drug interactions, contraception/folic acid, and reporting rash; document and arrange follow-up levels
opts.rat: #5 — Closing. Education and follow-up sustain safe therapy. RN role: teach + document.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each action, classify as APPROPRIATE or INAPPROPRIATE.

opts.text: The nurse infused the phenytoin in normal saline through an inline filter by pump at ≤50 mg/min with continuous cardiac and BP monitoring
opts.rat: Correct diluent, filter, rate, and monitoring prevent precipitation and fatal infusion reactions. Correct practice.

opts.text: The nurse used a large patent vein with blood return and monitored the site for pain, swelling, and discoloration
opts.rat: Protects against extravasation/purple glove syndrome. Correct practice.

opts.text: Given the albumin of 2.8, the nurse obtained a free phenytoin level and correlated it with the clinical exam
opts.rat: Low albumin makes the total level misleading; the free level guides interpretation. Correct practice.

opts.text: When the blood pressure dropped and the heart rate slowed during the load, the nurse slowed/stopped the infusion and supported the patient
opts.rat: Slowing/stopping for hypotension/bradycardia is the correct response to an infusion reaction. Correct practice.

opts.text: The nurse taught oral/dental hygiene for gingival hyperplasia, an additional contraceptive method, folic acid, and to report any rash or blistering
opts.rat: Addresses gingival hyperplasia, enzyme-inducer/teratogen risks, and SJS/DRESS reporting. Correct practice.

opts.text: The nurse mixed the phenytoin in D5W and infused it without an inline filter
opts.rat: ⚠️ Phenytoin precipitates in dextrose; saline only with an inline filter is required. Improper practice.

opts.text: To stop the seizure faster, the nurse gave a rapid IV push of undiluted phenytoin
opts.rat: ⚠️ Rapid administration causes hypotension, bradycardia, and asystole; infuse slowly (≤50 mg/min), never push. Improper practice.

opts.text: The nurse ignored the patient's complaint of arm pain and bluish discoloration at the IV site during the infusion
opts.rat: ⚠️ This signals extravasation/purple glove syndrome; the infusion must be stopped and the limb protected. Improper practice.

opts.text: The nurse told the patient a normal total level ruled out toxicity despite her low albumin, so no free level was needed
opts.rat: ⚠️ With low albumin the active free level can be high at a normal total; a free level and clinical correlation are needed. Improper practice.

opts.text: The nurse taught the patient she could stop the phenytoin on her own once she felt seizure-free
opts.rat: ⚠️ Abrupt discontinuation can precipitate status epilepticus; phenytoin is never stopped suddenly. Improper practice.
